serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate of noodle salad with a duck egg as typically served at a Burmese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle salad with egg — composite estimate derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cooked rice noodles, hard-boiled duck egg, sesame oil, fish sauce, onion, lime juice
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for တေးဘဲဥသုတ် (Tay Bae U Thoke) exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of FAO/INFOODS SEA entries for cooked rice noodles and USDA SR Legacy entries for hard-boiled duck egg, sesame oil, fish sauce, onion, and lime juice. Proportions estimated as approximately 55% noodles, 20% duck egg, 10% dressing/sauce, 15% vegetables and garnish. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Burmese noodle salad dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for တေးဘဲဥသုတ် (Tay Bae U Thoke). GI of 53 is estimated based on cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) as the dominant carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of duck egg, sesame oil, fish sauce, and lime juice does not substantially raise the GI; fat and protein content may modestly lower the effective GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.2, rounded to 34.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing about 42% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or soy sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 34 per standard serving is moderate-to-high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with favourable unsaturated fat profile from sesame oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 4.2 g fat per 100 g with a predominantly unsaturated fat profile from sesame oil, which is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, sodium from fish sauce should be monitored in patients with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: တေးဘဲဥသုတ် (romanized: Tay Bae U Thoke) is a traditional Burmese noodle salad dish featuring cooked noodles — typically rice noodles or thin wheat noodles — tossed with a hard-boiled or soft-boiled duck egg and dressed with a flavourful combination of sesame oil, fish sauce, lime juice, sliced onion, fresh chilli, coriander, and crispy fried shallots. The word 'thoke' (သုတ်) in Burmese refers to a hand-tossed salad, a culinary technique central to Burmese cuisine in which ingredients are mixed by hand to ensure even coating with the dressing. Duck egg (ဘဲဥ) is prized in Burmese cooking for its richer yolk and firmer white compared to chicken egg, contributing a deeper flavour and higher cholesterol content. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly chilled, making it a refreshing yet satisfying meal. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient from the noodle base, complemented by protein and fat from the duck egg and sesame oil. The sodium content is notable due to fish sauce. The dish is relevant in clinical nutrition contexts for its moderate glycemic load and sodium contribution. It is commonly served as a main meal or light lunch in Myanmar and at Burmese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for တေးဘဲဥသုတ် (Tay Bae U Thoke) exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved source entry exists for this specific Burmese dish. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a future approved regional food composition entry if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component sources used for this estimate. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in the traditional preparation of this dish. 5. The cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; this dish is a Burmese noodle salad and is not related to Singaporean Lor Mee. The tag has been disregarded. 6. Halal status is listed as false because fish sauce (ငါးပိရည်) used in traditional preparation may not be certified halal; individual restaurant preparation may vary. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in any approved source.
